course.title: Spanish: Out in the World
course.description: Practical Spanish for restaurants and travel — the situations where A1 vocabulary runs out first.

lessons.title: En el restaurante (At the restaurant)
lessons.content: Restaurant Spanish is highly scripted, which makes it an efficient place to gain fluency fast: the same dozen phrases cover almost every meal.
lessons.grammar_note: 'Quisiera' (I would like) is the polite workhorse here — it's the imperfect subjunctive of 'querer', softer than 'quiero' (I want) without needing any grammar you haven't met yet.
lessons.cultural_note: In Spain the bill almost never arrives unrequested; lingering at the table is expected, and you ask for 'la cuenta' when you're genuinely done.

vocabulary.example_sentence: Una mesa para dos, por favor.
Dejamos la propina en la mesa.

questions.question_text: What is the English translation of 'la cuenta'?
Complete the polite request: ___, por favor (the bill)
Put the words in order to ask for a table for two.

lessons.title: Los viajes (Travel)
lessons.content: Travel vocabulary is where prepositions start to matter: 'el tren a Madrid' versus 'el tren de Madrid' are opposite journeys.
lessons.grammar_note: 'Ir a' + infinitive is the near future: 'voy a viajar' (I'm going to travel). It's the shortcut that lets you talk about the future long before you learn the true future tense.
lessons.cultural_note: Spanish long-distance trains are punctual to the point of pride, and platform numbers ('el andén') often appear only minutes before departure.

vocabulary.example_sentence: El avión sale a las ocho.
La estación está cerca del hotel.
Compré un billete de ida y vuelta.
La llegada es a las diez.

questions.question_text: What is the English translation of 'el billete'?
Write the Spanish word for 'the station'.
Which phrase means 'I'm going to travel'?
